name: Palais de la Découverte, Paris
concept&appearance: The museum is housed in the Palais d'Antin, the West Wing of the historic Grand Palais, originally built for the 1900 Universal Exposition by architect Albert-Félix-Théophile Thomas in the Beaux-Arts style. The exterior features an ornate stone facade with colonnades and statues, while the interior is characterized by a monumental layout centered around a large elliptical rotunda with a glass dome. The design concept for the museum, established in 1937 by Jean Perrin, emphasizes 'science in the making' ('la science en train de se faire'), transforming the classic palace halls into interactive laboratories where experiments are performed live. The building blends late 19th-century architectural grandeur (mosaics, sculptures, glass roofs) with functional scientific exhibition spaces.
permanent_exhibitions: The museum is divided into several key scientific departments: Mathematics, Physics, Chemistry, Astronomy, Life Sciences, and Earth Sciences. 1. The Mathematics Department is famous for the 'Salle Pi' (Pi Room), a circular room where 707 digits of the number Pi are inscribed on the walls in wooden characters. 2. The Physics Department features the iconic Electrostatics Room, which hosts spectacular high-voltage demonstrations using a Van de Graaff generator, as well as sections on Nuclear Physics, Optics, and Mechanics. 3. The Chemistry Department offers a laboratory setting for experiments involving liquid nitrogen, reactions, and perfumes. 4. The Life Sciences Department includes the popular 'School of Rats' (École des Rats), showcasing animal behavior and learning, along with exhibits on human biology and communication. 5. The Earth Sciences Department covers geology, volcanology, and seismology. The core educational significance relies on 'exposés'—live experiments conducted by mediators rather than static displays.
central_hall: The 'Rotonde d'Antin' (Antin Rotunda) serves as the majestic central entrance hall. It is an elliptical space illuminated by a large glass dome (verrière) that floods the area with natural light. The hall features an elaborate mosaic floor with floral motifs and is decorated with allegorical sculptures representing the Arts. It acts as the monumental hub connecting the various exhibition wings and floors.
special_effects_theaters: The museum houses a Planetarium located beneath a 15-meter diameter dome. It is equipped with a projector (historically a Zeiss optical projector, modernized with digital systems) capable of simulating the starry sky, solar system movements, and celestial phenomena for an audience of approximately 200 people.
science_popularization_activities: The Palais de la Découverte is renowned for its oral mediation and daily live science shows called 'exposés'. These are interactive demonstrations led by 'scientific mediators' (formerly demonstrators) in lecture halls and laboratories, covering topics like electrostatics, cold temperatures (liquid nitrogen), and chemistry reactions. The museum also offers workshops, conferences with researchers, and educational programs for schools. A new 'Palais des Enfants' (Children's Palace) is designed to introduce children aged 2-10 to science through play and experimentation.
